elements.sent-2411-069: When you use \file{elsarticle.cls for formatting your submissions in the \verb+preprint+ mode, the document is formatted in single column style with a text width of 384pt or 5.3in.}
elements.sent-2411-070: When this document is formatted for final print and if the journal happens to be a double column journal, the text width will be reduced to 224pt at for 3+ double column and 5+ journals respectively.
elements.sent-2411-071: All the nifty fine-tuning in equation breaking done by the author goes to waste in such cases.
elements.sent-2411-072: Therefore, authors are requested to check this problem by typesetting their submissions in final format as well just to see if their equations are broken at appropriate places, by changing appropriate options in the document class loading command, which is explained in section~\ref{sec:usage , \nameref{sec:usage .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: This allows authors to fix any equation breaking problem before submission for publication.

elements.subsection-2411-002: Displayed equations and double column journals
elements.sent-2411-075: Many Elsevier journals print their text in two columns.
elements.sent-2411-076: Since the preprint layout uses a larger line width than such columns, the formulae are too wide for the line width in print.
elements.sent-2411-077: Here is an example of an equation (see equation 6) which is perfect in a single column preprint format: In normal course, articles are prepared and submitted in single column format even if the final printed article will come in a double column format journal.
elements.sent-2411-078: Here the problem is that when the article is typeset by the typesetters for paginating and fit within the single column width, they have to break the lengthy equations and align them properly.
elements.sent-2411-079: Even if most of the tasks in preparing your proof is automated, the equation breaking and aligning requires manual judgement, hence this task is manual.
elements.sent-2411-080: When there comes a manual operation that area is error prone.
elements.sent-2411-081: Author needs to check that equation pretty well.
elements.sent-2411-082: However if authors themselves break the equation to the single column width typesetters need not want to touch these area and the proof authors get will be without any errors.
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, authors may check their submissions for the incidence of such long equations and break the equations at the correct places so that the final typeset copy will be as they wish.

elements.sent-2411-087: In connection with cross-referencing and possible future hyperlinking it is not a good idea to collect more that one literature item in one \verb+\bibitem+.

elements.sent-2411-093: In the numbered scheme of citation, \verb+\cite{<label> + is used, since \verb+\citep+ or \verb+\citet+ has no relevance in the numbered scheme.}
elements.sent-2411-094: You can change this to author-year or harvard scheme by adding option \verb+authoryear+ in the class loading command.

elements.sent-2411-108: Multivariate time series classification is of great importance in practical applications and is a challenging task.
elements.sent-2411-109: However, deep neural network models such as Transformers exhibit high accuracy in multivariate time series classification but lack interpretability and fail to provide insights into the decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-110: On the other hand, traditional approaches based on decision tree classifiers offer clear decision processes but relatively lower accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-111: Swin Transformer (ST) addresses these issues by leveraging self-attention mechanisms to capture both fine-grained local patterns and global patterns.
elements.sent-2411-112: It can also model multi-scale feature representation learning, thereby providing a more comprehensive representation of time series features.
elements.sent-2411-113: To tackle the aforementioned challenges, we propose ST-Tree with interpretability for multivariate time series classification.
elements.sent-2411-114: Specifically, the ST-Tree model combines ST as the backbone network with an additional neural tree model.
elements.sent-2411-115: This integration allows us to fully leverage the advantages of ST in learning time series context while providing interpretable decision processes through the neural tree.
elements.sent-2411-116: This enables researchers to gain clear insights into the model's decision-making process and extract meaningful interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-117: Through experimental evaluations on 10 UEA datasets, we demonstrate that the ST-Tree model improves accuracy in multivariate time series classification tasks and provides interpretability through visualizing the decision-making process across different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-118: Multivariate time series classification is an important and challenging task for time series, which are sequences of measurements collected from sensors.
elements.sent-2411-119: A time series is a set of data in chronological order and is widely used in a variety of domains and in a variety of real-world applications, including atrial fibrillation detection (\cite{li2023diagnosis ), error message detection (\cite{Yu2022LSTMBasedID ), intelligent device detection \cite{Du2023AutomaticCI , sleep record classification \cite{Chambon2017ADL , drunk driving detection (\cite{Li2015DrunkDD ), human activity recognition (\cite{Ma2019AttnSenseMA ) and so on.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Time series classification is one of the important tasks in time series analysis.
elements.sent-2411-121: Time series classification methods predict labels for unknown time series instances from the knowledge gained from existing labeled instances.
elements.sent-2411-122: In recent years, more and more researchers focus on the time series classification problem, which has important research value in the field of data mining (\cite{Du2024MultivariateTS, HUANG2023868, CHEN2021126 ).}
elements.sent-2411-123: High accuracy is a concern for all researchers.
elements.sent-2411-124: However interpretability is still important in many tasks.
elements.sent-2411-125: While much work has been done on interpretability in computer vision, tabular data classification and natural language processing, research on time series has not received as much attention (\cite{Rojat2021ExplainableAI ).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Most methods are not directly applicable to time series due to the different structure and nature of time series data (\cite{Ismail2020BenchmarkingDL ).}
elements.sent-2411-127: In the early days of time series research, shapelets based methods were extensively studied (\cite{Ji2019AFS, Tao2022ProfilematrixbasedSD ).}
elements.sent-2411-128: Shapelets provide interpretability of time series data and can be used to characterize time series by identifying and extracting representative shape patterns (\cite{Ruiz2020TheGM ).}
elements.sent-2411-129: These shape patterns can be used to interpret the predictions of the model and thus enhance the understanding of the time series.
elements.sent-2411-130: However long time series data can cause the shapelet selection and extraction process to become more difficult, leading to increased computational complexity (\cite{Ji2019AFS ).}
elements.sent-2411-131: There are also dictionary-based TSC methods (\cite{Middlehurst2019ScalableDC ), classification decisions can be explained by the distribution of symbolic words.}
elements.sent-2411-132: However, this explanation is not intuitive enough because the distribution of symbolic words is difficult to judge directly.
elements.sent-2411-133: Some researchers can achieve high accuracy and interpretability through the combination of traditional shapelet and depth methods (\cite{Ji2022TimeSC, Ji2022FullyCN ).}
elements.sent-2411-134: However, these methods are not intuitive for the classification decision process.
elements.sent-2411-135: In recent years, a large number of deep model-based methods have been proposed, and commonly used models such as onvolutional Neural Network (CNN), Recurrent Neural Network (RNN), Fully Convolutional Network (FCN), Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Transformer and so on (\cite{Wang2016TimeSC, Turbe2022InterpretTimeAN, FU2024106234, WAN2024106196 ).}
elements.sent-2411-136: Among them, Transformer is a deep neural network model based on the self-attention mechanism with strong time series modeling capability.
elements.sent-2411-137: Through the self-attention mechanism, Transformer can capture global dependencies and local patterns in time series data and extract rich feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-138: Deep models are widely used for their end-to-end and classification high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-139: However, in numerous scenarios are in high-risk environments such as disease prediction or autonomous driving, the black-box characterization of these models is a significant drawback.
elements.sent-2411-140: Numerous approaches (\cite{Du2024MultivariateTS ) post-hoc interpretation such as gradcam, however, cannot reflect the decision-making process.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Thus it becomes crucial to utilize some method to reflect the interpretability of the decision process of the deep model.
elements.sent-2411-142: In multivariate time series classification, both deep neural network models and decision tree models have their advantages.
elements.sent-2411-143: However, two issues need to be addressed for these methods: (1) High accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-144: Deep neural network models learn complex feature representations by (\cite{Ismail2020BenchmarkingDL ), resulting in high accuracy rates.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Various methods based on deep models have emerged.
elements.sent-2411-146: The vast majority of deep models are black-box, post hoc explanatory models.. (2) Interpretability.
elements.sent-2411-147: The decision tree model is interpretable and can clearly show the decision-making process, making the results easier to understand and interpret (\cite{Theissler2022ExplainableAF,Kim2022ViTNeTIV ).}
elements.sent-2411-148: Both deep neural network models and decision tree models have their own advantages and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-149: In order to balance accuracy and interpretability, researchers have proposed methods that combine both (\cite{Pagliarini2022NeuralSymbolicTD ).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.7\textwidth]{example.pdf} \caption{ST-Tree's transparent decision-making process, with the original Epilepsy time series on the left, the tree structure in the middle, and the positions of the prototypes corresponding to the original time series is on the right.} \label{example}
elements.sent-2411-150: In response to the above challenges, we proposed ST-Tree with interpretability for multivariate time Series classification to deal with the problem of model interpretability while balancing high accuracy and an interpretable decision process.
elements.sent-2411-151: The method consists of two parts: the time patch module and the neural tree module.
elements.sent-2411-152: Specifically, the time patch module uses ST as a feature extractor and time patch generator.
elements.sent-2411-153: To improve the interpretability of the model and visualize the decision-making process, the generated time patches are fed into a tree model, the neural tree module, which hierarchically paths the multivariate time series through a binary tree.
elements.sent-2411-154: Finally, the leaf prediction module perform label prediction on the multivariate time series by calculating branch routing scores.
elements.sent-2411-155: The transparent decision process of ST-Tree is shown in Fig.~\ref{example , each node corresponds to a time series segment closest to the prototype.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The main contributions of this paper are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-157: We propose the ST-Tree model, which combined ST with an additional neural tree model, which can take full advantage of ST to learn the time series context, and can use neural tree to provide an interpretable decision process.
elements.sent-2411-158: The proposed neural tree model decision-making itself is transparent, and each node judgment criterion can be clearly understood through the prototypical solution of the nodes.
elements.sent-2411-159: The ST-Tree model is demonstrated to have improved accuracy on multivariate time series classification tasks through experimental evaluation on 10 UEA datasets.
elements.sent-2411-160: We visualize the tree model on different types of datasets and illustrate the good interpretability of the model through the decision-making process.
elements.sent-2411-161: The rest of this paper is organized as follows: Section 2 provides an overview of the related work in the field.
elements.sent-2411-162: In Section 3, we present our method in detail.
elements.sent-2411-163: Section 4 describes the sensitivity experiments conducted to validate our approach, comparing it with other state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-165: It also employs a sparse self-attention layer to extract long-range features and captures spatial correlation using Kendall coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-166: RT creates a new feature space representing the support for temporal rules using Allen interval algebra and shapelets as units.
elements.sent-2411-167: Dual-prototype shapes are generated, representing the overall shape and discriminative partial-length shapes of different classes.
elements.sent-2411-168: Ma et al. \cite{Ma2020AdversarialDS introduced ADSN, which employs an adversarial training strategy to generate shapelets that closely resemble the actual subsequence.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Shapelet transformation is then used to extract discriminative features.
elements.figure-2411-002: \caption{Parameters} \label{tabparameters} \begin{tabular}{cl} Parameter Meaning $d$ Tree depth, representing the maximum depth of the tree structure. $\theta$ Train parameters associated with the ST module. $\omega$ Train parameters associated with the tree module. $\mathcal{P}$ Prototype set, a collection of prototypes for similarity calculations. $T$ Initialized tree. $L$ Leaf node set, containing the IDs of the leaf nodes in the tree. $C $ Number of MTS channels dimension. $L'$ Length dimension of MTS. $c^a$ Convolution operation with filter size $a$. $i$ Current node index being traversed in the tree. $\mathbf{z}_i$ Input feature vector associated with node $i$. $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_i)$ Maximum similarity score between feature vector $\mathbf{z}_i$ and prototypes in $\mathcal{P}$. $\mathcal{R}_{i,j}(\mathbf{z}_i)$ Routing score from node $i$ to child node $j$, indicating the branching direction. $\mathcal{T}$ Training set used for training the model. $E$ Number of epochs for training. $f$ ST module function used for feature extraction. $B$ Number of batches. $\mathbf{z}_0$ Output time patch of the ST module. $\hat{y}$ Final predicted class label from the ST-Tree module. $N(\cdot)$ Set of nodes in the binary tree. $E_{i,j}(\cdot)$ Edge connecting parent node $i$ to child node $j$. $\mathbf{\tilde{z}}$ Reshaped time patch used for similarity calculations. $\mathcal{E}_{i,j}(\mathbf{z}_i)$ Function that transforms input feature $\mathbf{z}_i$ for node $i$ and child node $j$. $\mathbf{\rho}$ Vector of routing scores for each node, representing similarity with prototypes. $x$ In this study, x is used as an intermediate variable for time patch calculations. \end{tabular}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{overview.pdf} \caption{Schematic of ST-Tree classification via tree structure, with the original Epilepsy time series on the left, the tree structure in the middle. The nodes in the tree compute routing scores on the basis of the prototypes. In the figure, \( z_{0} - z_{6} \) represent the prototype of each node. \( R_{0, 1} - R_{2, 6} \) represent the probability of the left or right child node.} \label{overview}
elements.sent-2411-170: ST (\cite{Liu2021SwinTH ) brings certain advantages for processing multivariate time series.}
elements.sent-2411-171: It employs a patch-based processing strategy, dividing the time series data into parallelizable sub-sequences known as time patches, similar to patches in images.
elements.sent-2411-172: Additionally, by incorporating self-attention mechanism, it becomes possible to model the contextual information between different time steps in the series, enabling the capturing of temporal relationships and dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-173: To leverage these advantages, we propose the ST-Tree, illustrated in Fig.~\ref{overview .}
elements.sent-2411-174: It utilizes a time patch module (shown in Fig.~\ref{overview (a)) to extract time patches.}
elements.sent-2411-175: These time patches are then fed into the prototype-based neural tree module (depicted in Fig.~\ref{overview (b)), which enhances the interpretability of the model.}
elements.sent-2411-176: Algorithms \ref{algtree and \ref{algtraverse describe the training process for ST-Tree.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Training Process for ST-Tree} \label{algtree} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Training set $\mathcal{T}$, number of epochs $E$, ST module $f$, ST parameters $\theta$, tree model parameters $\omega$ \For{$e \in\{1, \ldots, E\}$} \State Split $\mathcal{T}$ into $B$ batches $\{\mathcal{T}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{T}_B\}$ \Comment{Batch splitting process} \For{batch $b \in \{1, \ldots, B\}$} \State Extract batch $\left(x_b, y_b\right)$ from $\mathcal{T}_b$ \State $\mathbf{z}_0^b \gets f(x_b; \theta)$ \Comment{Output from the ST module} \State $\hat{y}_b \gets \Call{traverse}{(1, \mathbf{z}_0^b; \omega)}$ \Comment{Traverse the tree to get predictions} \State $\text{loss}(\hat{y}_b, y_b)$ \Comment{Calculate the loss based on predictions} \State Update $\theta$ and $\omega$ \Comment{Optimize model parametersusing Adam optimizer} \EndFor \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-177: We optimize a ST-based architecture, specifically the time patch module, for efficient feature extraction and obtaining time patches.
elements.sent-2411-178: The structure of the time patch module is illustrated in Fig.~ \ref{timepatch .}
elements.sent-2411-179: Time partition module is utilized as the first step to segment and obtain the initial time patch.
elements.sent-2411-180: The multivariate time series $X$ of dimensions $(T \times C)$ is reshaped using four non-overlapping neighboring timestamps to generate a time patch, which is embedded with dimensions $\frac{T {4n \times 4nC$.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: The attention module (detailed in Section ~\ref{att ) is employed as the second step for feature refinement.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Embedding the attention module into the self-attention structure helps it to obtain more differentiated time domain features and time patches.
elements.sent-2411-183: Finally, the features pass through a Layer Normalization (LN) layer and an Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) layer for further processing.
elements.sent-2411-184: The self-attention calculation formula is shown in Eq.~\eqref{eq1 .}
elements.sent-2411-185: The query ($Q$), key ($K$) and value ($V$) are the matrices by extracting features from attention module (detailed in Section \ref{att ). \begin{equation}\label{eq1} X_{SA}=softmax\left(\frac{attention _{Q} X\left( attention _{K} X\right)^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) attention_{V} X\end{equation} SHIFT module.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} X_{SA}=softmax\left(\frac{attention _{Q} X\left( attention _{K} X\right)^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}}\right) attention_{V} X\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-186: It incorporates a shift-window mechanism to limit the self-attention computation to non-overlapping local temporal patch windows.
elements.sent-2411-187: This design choice enables the interaction of attention between individual time patches.
elements.sent-2411-188: After obtaining patches from the Time Patch Module in Section \ref{timepatchsec , the next steps involve initializing a tree.}
elements.sent-2411-189: These prototypes will correspond to each node in the tree. \begin{equation}\text{chunked\_prototypes} = \text{chunk}(\text{prototypes},~\text{num\_prototypes}) \label{prototype_chunking}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-190: For each node in the tree, the output is computed as Eq. \ref{tree_traversal , where \(\text{logits}\) is the prediction of ST model.}
elements.sent-2411-191: The out is the final prediction result. \begin{equation}\text{out} = \text{TreeTraversal}(\text{logits},~ \text{patches}',~\text{chunked\_prototypes}) \label{tree_traversal}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-192: Let \( T(i, d) \) be the tree initialization function where \( i \) is the index and \( d \) is the current depth.
elements.sent-2411-193: The formula for tree traversal is given in Eq. \ref{eqtree_initialization , with detailed steps outlined in Algorithm \ref{algtraverse . \begin{equation}T(i, d) = \begin{cases} \text{Leaf}(i, \text{num\_classes}) \text{if } d = \text{tree\_depth} \text{Branch}(i, \, T(i + 1, d + 1), \, T(i + \text{left.size} + 1, d + 1), \, \text{prototypes}) \text{if } d < \text{tree\_depth} \end{cases} \label{eqtree_initialization}\end{equation} where \text{Leaf (i, \text{num\_classes ) generates a leaf node with index \( i \) and number of classes when \( d \) reaches the maximum \text{tree\_depth .}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}T(i, d) =  Leaf(i, num\_classes) if  d = tree\_depth Branch(i, \, T(i + 1, d + 1), \, T(i + left.size + 1, d + 1), \, prototypes) if  d < tree\_depth  \label{eqtree_initialization}\end{equation}
elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Pseudocode for Traverse Tree through Recursive
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Traverse a Tree with Routing Scores} \label{algtraverse} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Tree depth $d$, ST model parameter $\theta$, tree parameter $\omega$, prototype set $\mathcal{P}$ \Ensure A traversed tree with routing scores \State $T \gets \text{init\_tree}(d, \omega)$ \Comment Initialize tree with depth $d$ and parameter $\omega$ \State Leaf node set $L \gets \emptyset$ \Comment Initialize the leaf node set as empty \State $L \gets \text{assign\_leaf\_nodes}(T)$ \Comment Assign node IDs of tree $T$ to the leaf node set $L$ \Function {traverse}{$i, \mathbf{z}_{i}$} \Comment Recursively traverse the tree, starting from node $i$ and input feature $\mathbf{z}_{i}$ \If{$i \in L$} \Comment If node $i$ is a leaf node \State \Return $\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{z}_{i})$ \Comment Return class probabilities obtained through FC layers \EndIf \State $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) \gets \max(\text{similarity}(\mathbf{z}_{i}, \mathcal{P}))$ \Comment Calculate the maximum similarity between node $i$'s feature $\mathbf{z}_{i}$ and the prototype set $\mathcal{P}$ \State $\mathcal{R}_{i, 2i}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) \gets \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_{i})$ \Comment Routing score for the left child node (index $2i$) \State $\mathcal{R}_{i, 2i+1}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) \gets 1 - \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_{i})$ \Comment Routing score for the right child node (index $2i+1$) \State $l_{\text{child}} \gets \Call{traverse}{2i, \mathcal{E}_{i, 2i}(\mathbf{z}_{i})}$ \Comment Recursively traverse the left child node \State $r_{\text{child}} \gets \Call{traverse}{2i+1, \mathcal{E}_{i, 2i+1}(\mathbf{z}_{i})}$ \Comment Recursively traverse the right child node \State \Return $\mathcal{R}_{i, 2i}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) \times l_{\text{child}} + \mathcal{R}_{i, 2i+1}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) \times r_{\text{child}}$ \Comment Return the weighted sum of the left and right child's routing scores \EndFunction \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-194: The prototype feature vector serves as a representative of a time series category.
elements.sent-2411-195: When discriminative features are sampled, they are routed to child nodes based on a routing direction determined by a routing module.
elements.sent-2411-196: Specifically, each tree node corresponds to a trainable prototype $\mathcal{P _i$ for measuring the routing score $\mathcal{N _i \in [0,1]$ for the reshaped time patch $\mathbf{z}$, as shown in Fig.~\ref{overview (b).}}}
elements.sent-2411-197: We introduce a module that uses perfect binary trees and a routing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-198: The module consists of sets of nodes $N(\cdot)$, leaf nodes $L(\cdot)$, and edges $E_{i,j (\cdot)$ connecting parent node $i$ to child node $j$.}
elements.sent-2411-199: This module represents a perfect binary tree, where each internal node has two children: $N_{2 \times i}$ and $N_{2 \times i+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-200: Given the encoder output $\mathbf{z_0 = f(x;\theta)$, the module predicts the final class label $\hat{y}$ using a soft decision-making process.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Computing L2 Distance for Similarity ST-Tree computes the squared Euclidean distance between the time patch and the prototype of each category (as described in Eq. \ref{square_operation -\ref{eqsimilarity ).}}
elements.sent-2411-202: This distance is considered as the similarity score between the time patch and the category prototype, where a smaller distance value indicates a higher similarity score.
elements.sent-2411-203: To quantify the similarity between the prototype and the time patch in each node, the similarity score is determined as in (\cite{Chen2018ThisLL ).}
elements.sent-2411-204: The distance is measured using a convolutional operation, where each prototype acts as a convolutional kernel on $\mathbf{z}$.
elements.sent-2411-205: The distance between the prototype and $\mathbf{z}$ is computed, and we represent the similarity by using a logarithmic similarity measure as briefly defined in Eq.~\eqref{eq2 , detailed in \ref{square_operation -\ref{eqsimilarity . \begin{equation}\label{eq2} \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{z}_{i}) = \log\left(1 + \max_{\tilde{\mathbf{z}} \in \text{patches}(\mathbf{z}_{i})} \frac{1}{\|\tilde{\mathbf{z}} - \mathcal{P}_{i}\|_{2}^{2} + \epsilon}\right)\end{equation} Assume that the prototype vector has dimensions of \( [1, 1, k] \), where \( k \) is less than the length \( L' \) of the time series.}}}
elements.sent-2411-206: The time patch vector \( x \) has dimensions \( [B, C, L'] \).
elements.sent-2411-207: The detailed steps to compute the L2 distance are as follows: \begin{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-208: Square the input time patch features $x$ as Eq. \ref{square_operation . \begin{equation}x^2_{i,j} = (x_{i,j})^2 \label{square_operation}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-209: Use convolution to compute the sum of squares within a local region as Eq. \ref{eqconvolution_sum_squares .}
elements.sent-2411-210: In Eq. \ref{eqconvolution_sum_squares , \( k \) is the length of the convolution kernel.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Compute the sum of squares of the prototype vector as Eq. \ref{eqsum_squares_proto_vector .}
elements.sent-2411-212: In Eq. \ref{eqsum_squares_proto_vector , \(\text{proto\_vector _{k}\) is element of the prototype vector at position \(k\). \begin{equation}p2 = \sum_{k=0}^{H-1} (\text{proto\_vector}_{k})^2 \label{eqsum_squares_proto_vector}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Compute convolution similarity, use convolution to compute the dot product between the feature vector and the prototype vector as Eq. \ref{eqconvolution_similarity . \begin{equation}\text{xp}_{i,j} = \sum_{k=0}^{L'-1} x_{i,j+k} \cdot \text{proto\_vector}_{k} \label{eqconvolution_similarity}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Compute the L2 distance, combine the sum of squares and the dot product to get the final L2 distance as Eq. \ref{l2_distance .}
elements.sent-2411-215: In Eq. \ref{l2_distance , \(\text{x2\_patch\_sum _{i,j}\) is sum of squared values of the input feature \(x\) over a local patch.}}
elements.sent-2411-217: The routing score determines the branching direction (left or right), as shown in Fig.\ref{overview (b).}
elements.sent-2411-218: We define the routing score for each child node as briefly described in Eq.\eqref{eq3 , detailed in Eq. \ref{maxim -\ref{final_dists . \begin{equation}\label{eq3} \mathcal{R}_{i, j}\left(\mathbf{z}_i\right) = \begin{cases} \left[\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{z}_i\right)\right]_0^1 \text{if } j = 2 \times i 1 - \left[\mathcal{N}\left(\mathbf{z}_i\right)\right]_0^1 \text{if } j = 2 \times i + 1 \end{cases}\end{equation} \end{flushleft.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\label{eq3} R_{i, j}\left(z_i\right) =  \left[N\left(z_i\right)\right]_0^1 if  j = 2 \times i 1 - \left[N\left(z_i\right)\right]_0^1 if  j = 2 \times i + 1 \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-219: In Eq. \ref{maxim , \(\text{similarity}\) is similarity scores computed from the distance. \begin{equation}\text{maxim} = \text{maxpool1d}(\text{similarity}) \label{maxim}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In Eq. \ref{eq:to_left nd \ref{eq:to_right , \(\text{to\_left}\) is probability of choosing the left branch, to\_left takes the values from the 1st column of all rows.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: In Eq. \ref{eqldis and \ref{eqrdis , \(\text{logits}\) is prediction from ST model.}}
elements.sent-2411-222: In Eq. \ref{final_dists , \(\text{dists}\) is the computed distance values in current node, combining distance information from both the left and right branches.}
elements.sent-2411-224: We propose an attention module that utilizes spatial and channel attention mechanisms for feature refinement and extraction.
elements.sent-2411-225: We introduce the context converter module to capture more distinct time patches.
elements.sent-2411-226: Finally, the context time patch $\mathbf{z_i}$ enhanced by attention module ($V_{i,j}$) to get $\mathbf{z_j}$ using Eq.~\eqref{eq6 , and next $\mathbf{z_j}$ will be fed into the corresponding child nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Assume that the dimension of patch is $B \times C \times L'$.
elements.sent-2411-228: The structure of attention module is shown in Fig.~\ref{overview (c).}
elements.sent-2411-229: Channel Attention: the spatial information of the feature graph is first aggregated using average and maximum pooling operations to generate two different spatial context descriptors representing the average and maximum pooling features, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-231: The channel attention is computed as Eq.~\eqref{eq4 .}
elements.sent-2411-232: In Eq.~\eqref{eq4 , the dimension of $p$ is $B \times C \times 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Spatial Attention: the relationship between feature spaces is utilized to generate a spatial attention map, which is complementary to channel attention.
elements.sent-2411-234: The average pooling operation and the maximum pooling operation are first applied along the channel axis generate a valid feature descriptor.
elements.sent-2411-235: The spatial attention is calculated as Eq.~\eqref{eq5 .}
elements.sent-2411-236: In Eq.~\eqref{eq5 , the dimension of $p_j$ is $B \times 1 \times L'$.}
elements.sent-2411-237: In Eq.~\eqref{eq4 and Eq.~\eqref{eq5 , $c^a$ is one-dimensional convolution operation with filter size $a$. \begin{equation}\label{eq4} q = \operatorname{sigmoid}\left(c^a\left[\operatorname{avgpool1d}\left(q_i\right) ; \operatorname{maxpool1d}\left(q_i\right)\right]\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq5} q_j = \operatorname{sigmoid}\left(c^a\left[\operatorname{avgpool1d}(q) ; \operatorname{maxpool1d}(q)\right]\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eq6} \mathbf{z}_j = V_{i,j}\left(\mathbf{z}_i\right) = \operatorname{attention}\left(\mathbf{z}_i\right)\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-238: The leaf node in each tree module corresponds to the leaf prediction module $\mathcal{L (\cdot)$ used to predict the category probabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-239: Let $\mathbf{\rho(z_0)}$ be the cumulative routing score of the output $\mathbf{z_0}$ from the root node to the $l$-th leaf node on a set of edges of a particular path $p_l$.
elements.sent-2411-240: The cumulative routing score $\mathbf{\rho(z_i)}$ is computed by using Eq.~\eqref{eqpred .}
elements.sent-2411-241: In Eq. \ref{eqpred , \(\mathcal{R _{i,j}\) indicates the routing from node \(i\) to child node \(j\), detailed in Eq. \ref{eq3 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Each leaf prediction module passes through the time patch fully connected layer (timeFC), and tree fully connected layer (treeFC), as shown in Fig.~\ref{overview (d).}
elements.sent-2411-243: The leaf prediction module is computed as Eq.~\eqref{eq8 .}
elements.sent-2411-244: In Eq.~\eqref{eq8 , treeFC is obtained based on a certain path, and timeFC is obtained through time patch module.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Thus, the module can combine local path and global contextual feature representations.
elements.sent-2411-246: The detailed steps are shown as Eq. \ref{eqx - \ref{eqdistsfinal . \begin{equation}\label{eq8} \begin{aligned} \begin{gathered} \mathcal{L}\left(\mathbf{z}_l, x\right)=\operatorname{treeFC}\left(\mathbf{z}_l\right)+\operatorname{timeFC}(\mathbf{z}_0) \end{gathered} \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-247: Use max pooling to process the features of the nodes in the layer preceding the leaf as Eq. \ref{eqx .}
elements.sent-2411-248: In Eq. \ref{eqx , $\text{patches ''$ is the feature from the layer preceding the leaf. \begin{equation}x = \text{maxpool1d}(\text{patches}'') \label{eqx}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-249: Obtain the final prediction through path prediction and ST prediction as Eq. \ref{eqx .}
elements.sent-2411-250: In Eq. \ref{eqx , \(\text{pred (x)\) are predictions obtained from a MLP layer in the leaf node, logits are predictions of ST module. \begin{equation}\text{dists}' = \text{pred}(x) + \text{logits} \label{eqdists}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-251: Use softmax function to obtain the final prediction as Eq. \ref{eqx .}
elements.sent-2411-252: In \ref{eqx , \(\text{pred (x)\) are predictions obtained from a MLP layer in the leaf node, logits are predictions of ST module. \begin{equation}\text{dists\_leaf} = \text{softmax}(\text{dists}' ) \label{eqdistsfinal}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-253: The ultimate prediction $\hat{y}$ is obtained through the multiplication of all leaf predictions $\mathcal{L}$ with the sum of the accumulated routing scores $\rho_l$, as illustrated in Eq.~\eqref{eqloss1 -Eq.~\eqref{eqloss4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-254: The optimization of $\hat{y}$ is achieved by utilizing the Cross-Entropy loss function with the true label $y$.
elements.sent-2411-255: The final prediction $\hat{y}$ is computed by multiplying all leaf predictions $\mathcal{L}$ by the sum of the accumulated routing scores $\rho_l$, as shown in Eq.~\eqref{eqloss1 -Eq.~\eqref{eqloss4 . \begin{equation}\label{eqloss1} \hat{y} = \mathbf{\rho}^T \cdot \mathbf{g}(x)\end{equation} \begin{equation}g_l(x) = \sigma\left(\mathcal{L}(x, \mathbf{z}_l)\right)\end{equation} \begin{equation}\mathbf{\rho} = [\rho_1(\mathbf{z}_1), \rho_2(\mathbf{z}_2), \dots, \rho_n(\mathbf{z}_n)]\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{eqloss4} \mathbf{g}(x) = [g_1(x), g_2(x), \dots, g_n(x)]\end{equation} In Eq. \ref{eqloss1 , $\mathbf{\rho}$ is a vector of routing scores for each node $l$, representing the similarity between each time patch $\mathbf{z _l$ and its prototype.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-256: The loss function for leaf nodes to make predictions is shown in Eq.~\eqref{eq10 .}
elements.sent-2411-257: In Eq.~\eqref{eq10 , $N$ is the training sample set, $M$ is the number of labels, $y$ is the true label, and $\hat{y}$ is the model prediction label. \begin{equation}\label{eq10} \begin{aligned} \begin{gathered} Loss=-\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \sum_{j=1}^M y_{i, j} \log p\left(\hat{y}_{i, j}\right) \end{gathered} \end{aligned}\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-258: These datasets are mainly categorized as: Motion, Electroencephalography/Magnetoencephalography (EEG/MEG), Human Activity Recognition (HAR), Audio Spectra (AS).
elements.sent-2411-259: More information of each dataset are shown in Table~\ref{tab1 .}
elements.figure-2411-005: [] \caption{Brief information regarding the 10 benchmark datasets.} \label{tab1} \begin{tabular}{lllllll} Datasets Type Train Test Dimensions Lengths Classes AWR Motion 275 300 9 144 25 AF ECG 15 15 2 640 3 BM HAR 40 40 6 100 4 CT Motion 1422 1436 3 182 20 \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}HMD\end{tabular} EEG/MEG 160 74 10 400 4 NATOPS HAR 180 180 24 51 6 PD Motion 7494 3498 2 8 10 SR2 EEG/MEG 200 180 7 1152 2 SAD AS 6599 2199 13 93 10 SWJ ECG 12 15 4 2500 3 \end{tabular}
elements.sent-2411-261: ArticularyWordRecognition (AWR): The AWR dataset comprises 12 sensors providing X, Y, and Z time series positions at a sampling rate of 200 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-262: AtrialFibrillation (AF): The AF dataset consists of two-channel ECG recordings aimed at predicting spontaneous termination of AF.
elements.sent-2411-263: Each instance in the dataset represents a 5-second segment of AF, containing two ECG signals sampled at a rate of 128 samples per second.
elements.sent-2411-264: BasicMotions (BM): The BM dataset was generated by four students performing four different activities while wearing a smartwatch.
elements.sent-2411-265: It includes four classes: walking, resting, running, and badminton.
elements.sent-2411-266: CharacterTrajectories (CT): The CT dataset was captured using a tablet and includes three dimensions: x-coordinate, y-coordinate, and pen tip force.
elements.sent-2411-267: The data was recorded at a sampling rate of 200 Hz.
elements.sent-2411-268: HandMovementDirection (HMD): The HMD dataset includes MEG activity modulated based on the direction of wrist movements.
elements.sent-2411-269: The data was recorded while subjects performed wrist movements in four different directions.
elements.sent-2411-270: NATOPS: The NATOPS dataset is generated using sensors placed on the hands, elbows, wrists, and thumbs.
elements.sent-2411-271: It records the x, y, and z coordinates for each of the eight sensor locations.
elements.sent-2411-272: PenDigits: The PenDigits dataset is used for handwritten digit classification.
elements.sent-2411-273: It involves 44 different writers drawing digits from 0 to 9.
elements.sent-2411-274: Each instance in the dataset consists of the x and y coordinates of the pen as it traces the digit on a digital screen.
elements.sent-2411-275: SelfRegulationSCP2 (SR2): The SR2 dataset was obtained from an artificially respirated patient.
elements.sent-2411-276: The subject controlled the movement of a cursor on a computer screen while cortical potentials were recorded.
elements.sent-2411-277: SpokenArabicDigits (SAD): The SAD dataset comprises 8800 time series instances representing spoken Arabic digits.
elements.sent-2411-278: Each instance is composed of 13 Frequency Cepstral Coefficients extracted from the speech signals.
elements.sent-2411-279: StandWalkJump (SWJ): The SWJ dataset includes short-duration ECG signals recorded from a healthy 25-year-old male performing various physical activities.
elements.sent-2411-280: The dataset aims to study the effect of motion artifacts on ECG signals and their sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-281: All models were trained in a Python 3.8 environment using PyTorch 1.10.0 with Cuda 11.3.
elements.sent-2411-282: The training environment was configured with the Ubuntu 20.04 operating system, equipped with an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 GPU (20GB VRAM) and an Intel(R) Xeon(R) Platinum 8352V CPU (48GB RAM).
elements.sent-2411-283: For all experiments in this paper, ST-Tree utilized a three-layer neural tree module and a one-layer time patch module.
elements.sent-2411-284: The model was trained using the Adam optimizer and a Cross-Entropy loss function.
elements.sent-2411-285: The results reported in this study were obtained after 50 epoches on all datasets.
elements.sent-2411-286: To address model performance sensitivity to hyperparameters:.
elements.sent-2411-287: When using the Adam optimizer to update model parameters, the initial learning rate is set to 0.001, which controls the step size of weight updates.
elements.sent-2411-288: The learning rate decay period is set to 5, meaning that the learning rate will decrease if the loss does not improve after every 5 training epochs.
elements.sent-2411-289: The decay factor is set to 0.90, indicating that the learning rate will be reduced to 90\% of its original value after every update.
elements.sent-2411-290: This exponential decay strategy improves the model's convergence and stability by gradually reducing the learning rate, allowing for finer adjustments as the model approaches the optimal solution.
elements.sent-2411-291: The batch size must fit within our GPU memory.
elements.sent-2411-292: Through testing, we found 50 epochs are sufficient for the model to converge.
elements.sent-2411-293: Model Parameters The ST-Tree employs a three-layer neural tree module and a one-layer time patch module.
elements.sent-2411-294: For the decision tree model, the depth affects the model's complexity and performance.
elements.sent-2411-295: We have discussed the impact of tree depth on model accuracy in Section \ref{sectreedepth .}
elements.sent-2411-296: For the time patch module, we use only one layer because the time patch relies on self-attention calculations, which have significant time and space complexity.
elements.sent-2411-297: Using multiple layers could lead to overfitting and difficulties in convergence, while one layer has already shown sufficient performance, as indicated in column ST-Tree (without tree) of Table \ref{tab3 .}
elements.sent-2411-298: Therefore, for training convenience and performance, we use just one layer.
elements.sent-2411-299: This allows for independent replication of the experimental results.
elements.sent-2411-300: The accuracies of these methods, along with their corresponding parameters from publicly available data in their respective studies, are presented in Table~\ref{tab2 .}
elements.sent-2411-301: The following is a brief description of the methods involved in the comparison.
elements.sent-2411-302: MLSTM-FCN: Combines the LSTM, FCN, and Attention blocks to enhance accuracy in MTSC by incorporating a squeeze-and-excitation block into the FCN block.
elements.sent-2411-303: ShapeNet: Projects MTS subsequences into a unified space and applies clustering to identify shapelets.
elements.sent-2411-304: Rocket, MiniRocket: Utilize random convolutional kernels to extract features from univariate time series, with extensions made for MTSC.
elements.sent-2411-305: TNC: Exploits the local smoothness of a signal to define temporal neighborhoods and learn generalizable representations.
elements.sent-2411-306: TS-TCC: Promotes consistency among different data augmentations to learn transformation-invariant representations.
elements.sent-2411-307: MTSC\_FF: Extracts fusion features by leveraging Transformer, GNN, and Attention mechanism modules to capture time domain spatial and frequency domain features, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-308: TST(S), TST(U): Processes the values at each timestamp as input for a transformer encoder, Incorporates unsupervised and unsupervised pre-training scheme within the framework relatively.
elements.sent-2411-309: SRL: Utilizes negative sampling techniques with an encoder-based architecture to learn representations.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{cd} \caption{CD diagram of 9 implementations on 10 UEA datasets. } \label{cd}
elements.sent-2411-310: Based on the results provided in Table~\ref{tab2 , we clearly observe that MTSC\_FF has the highest average accuracy (0.789 ) on the 10 datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-311: To visualize the comparison results more, we show the critical difference figure (CD) based on the accuracies in Table~\ref{tab2 .}
elements.sent-2411-312: The CD figure ranks the 12 MTS classification classifiers in ascending order.
elements.sent-2411-313: As shown in Fig.~\ref{cd , ST-Tree has the smallest rank.}
elements.sent-2411-314: The CD figure similarly illustrates that our method is in the first tier.
elements.sent-2411-315: ST-Tree exceeds the other methods in the dataset StandWalkJump, which, based on the characteristics of this dataset, indicates that ST-Tree is more capable of modeling long sequences.
elements.sent-2411-316: Among them, MTSC\_FF, MF-Net use self-attention and graph neural network to mine global, spatial and other features.
elements.sent-2411-317: ST-Tree is different from MF-Net and MTSC\_FF in terms of methodology and feature mining, the latter focuses on modeling global and spatial features, which is suitable for datasets with high dimensionality.
elements.sent-2411-318: ShapeNet projects the time series subsequence into a uniform space and applies clustering to find shape features.
elements.sent-2411-319: ST-Tree differs from ShapeNet in terms of methodology and feature extraction, as ST-Tree is a tree-based approach while ShapeNet uses clustering and shapelets features.
elements.sent-2411-320: Shapelets are precise and intuitive, while ST-Tree can visualize the decision process while being interpretable.
elements.sent-2411-321: TST takes the value of each timestamp as an input to the Transformer encoder.
elements.sent-2411-322: ST-Tree is a method based on ST combined with a tree structure, whereas TStamp Transformer uses the Transformer encoder and is not able to visualize the decision process.
elements.figure-2411-007: [] \caption{Experimental results from traditional and deep-learning implementations. Avg represents the average accuracy achieved by each method across 10 datasets and Win indicates the number of datasets for which a method achieved the best accuracy. The highest accuracy for each dataset is marked in bold.} \label{tab2} 1.1\textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{lllllllllllll} Dataset \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}MTSC \_FF\end{tabular} TST(S) ROCKET \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Shape Net\end{tabular} SRL \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}Mini Rocket\end{tabular} TST(U) \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}TSC -TCC\end{tabular} TNC \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}MLSTM -FCN\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}1NN -DTW\end{tabular} \begin{tabular}[c]{@{}l@{}}ST -tree\end{tabular} AWR 0.983 0.977 0.996 0.987 0.987 0.992 0.983 0.953 0.973 0.973 0.980 0.980 AF 0.533 0.067 0.249 0.400 0.133 0.133 0.200 0.267 0.133 0.267 0.267 0.466 BM 0.950 0.975 0.990 1.000 1.000 1.000 0.975 1.000 0.975 0.950 1.000 0.950 CT 0.986 0.975 0.967 0.980 0.994 0.993 N/A 0.985 0.967 0.985 0.969 0.970 HMD 0.541 0.243 0.446 0.338 0.270 0.392 0.608 0.243 0.324 0.365 0.306 0.554 NATOPS 0.889 0.850 0.885 0.883 0.944 0.928 0.900 0.822 0.911 0.889 0.850 0.916 PD 0.979 0.560 0.996 0.977 0.983 N/A 0.974 0.974 0.979 0.978 0.939 0.980 SR2 0.511 0.589 0.533 0.578 0.556 0.522 0.589 0.533 0.550 0.472 0.533 0.550 SAD 0.990 0.993 0.712 0.975 0.956 0.620 0.993 0.970 0.934 0.990 0.960 0.990 SWJ 0.467 0.267 0.456 0.533 0.400 0.333 0.267 0.333 0.400 0.067 0.333 0.533 Avg 0.783 0.650 0.723 0.765 0.722 0.657 0.721 0.708 0.715 0.694 0.714 0.789 Win 1 2 2 1 3 1 3 1 0 0 1 1 \end{tabular} }
elements.sent-2411-323: The neural tree module and the attention module were removed for validating the effectiveness of each module, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-324: ST-Tree obtained the best "Win" (9) and the highest average accuracy (0.789).
elements.sent-2411-325: As shown in Fig.~\ref{ab , the experimental results illustrate the positive effects of the neural Tree module and the attention module for ST-Tree.}
elements.figure-2411-008: \subfloat[ST-Tree (without tree)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{ab1} \label{ab1} } \subfloat[ST-Tree (without attention)]{ \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{ab2} \label{ab2} } \caption{Comparison of the accuracy of ST-Tree and ST-Tree (without tree), ST-Tree (without attention), the closer to the upper left corner ST-Tree works better.} \label{ab}
elements.sent-2411-326: We visualized the decision-making process during classification on the BasicMotions, CharacterTrajectories and AtrialFibrillation datasets, as shown in Fig.~\ref{treeCha , \ref{treebasic , \ref{treeAtr and \ref{treeAWR ..}}}}
elements.sent-2411-327: The orange parts of the figure are the prototype bases corresponding to the tree nodes, and the arrows represent the decision order.
elements.sent-2411-328: We analyzed the model and tree depth against the ArticularyWordRecognition, HandMovementDirection, NATOPS and SelfRegulationSCP2 datasets.
elements.sent-2411-329: The experimental results show that we can see that the tree depth of the ST-Tree varies with the complexity of the dataset and that increasing the depth does not improve the performance but leads to overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-330: According to Fig.~\ref{treedepth , we can analyze the effect of the number of layers of the tree corresponding to different datasets on the accuracy.}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.6\textwidth]{treedepth} \caption{ Variation in the precision of the number of layers of the corresponding tree for different datasets.} \label{treedepth}
elements.sent-2411-331: For Articulatory Word Recognition, as the number of layers in the tree increases from 3 to 6, accuracy decreases.
elements.sent-2411-332: Therefore, increasing the tree's depth results in reduced accuracy, making a tree depth of 4 layers preferable.
elements.sent-2411-333: For HandMovementDirection, increasing the number of layers in the tree does not enhance accuracy; instead, the accuracy remains stable regardless of the number of layers.
elements.sent-2411-334: This may indicate that the number of tree layers does not have a significant effect on the accuracy in this particular dataset.
elements.sent-2411-335: For NATOPS, the accuracy improved when the number of layers in the tree was increased from 3 to 4.
elements.sent-2411-336: This shows that increasing the number of layers in the tree can improve the accuracy, but further increasing the number of layers may not have a significant effect.
elements.sent-2411-337: Therefore, it is better to keep the tree at 4 layers.
elements.sent-2411-338: For SelfRegulationSCP2, the increase in the number of layers of the tree leads to a continuous decrease, so a 3-layer tree works best.
elements.sent-2411-339: In summary, when choosing the number of layers in the tree, the specific dataset characteristics and performance requirements need to be considered.
elements.sent-2411-340: Our method combats noise in the following ways.
elements.sent-2411-341: We implement data preprocessing steps, such as normalization and standardization.
elements.sent-2411-342: This processing enhances the overall quality of the data, making the model more effective during training.
elements.sent-2411-343: By using the self-attention mechanism, the model can automatically focus on the most relevant parts of the input sequence, reducing the impact of noise on the decision-making process while learning good representations of the time series.
elements.sent-2411-344: This mechanism allows the model to extract useful features even in noisy environments.
elements.sent-2411-346: The convolution layer computes the sum of squares of input features, which amplifies the characteristics of the true signal while reducing noise interference.
elements.sent-2411-347: Combined with the ReLU activation function, it effectively removes negative values, further enhancing the robustness of the model.
elements.sent-2411-348: To reduce training and computational costs, we propose the following strategies, the detailed process is shown in Algorithm \ref{algtransfer .}
elements.sent-2411-350: Monitor the performance on the validation set during training.
elements.sent-2411-351: If there is no significant improvement over several training epochs, stop training early.
elements.sent-2411-353: By dynamically adjusting the learning rate, we can converge to the optimal solution more quickly.
elements.sent-2411-354: In the phase of obtaining time series representations, leverage the knowledge of pre-trained models by fine-tuning them to adapt to new tasks.
elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Training Process for ST-Tree with Enhanced Strategies and Transfer Learning} \label{algtransfer} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \Require Training set $\mathcal{T}$, number of epochs $E$, ST module $f$, ST parameters $\theta$, tree model parameters $\omega$, validation set $\mathcal{V}$, patience $P$, initial learning rate $\alpha$, pre-trained parameters $\theta_{pre}$ , $decay\_steps=5, decay\_rate = 0.90$ \State Initialize $learning\_rate$ \State Initialize best validation performance $best\_val\_perf$ and counter $patience\_counter = 0$ \State Load pre-trained parameters $\theta \gets \theta_{pre}$ \Comment{Transfer learning initialization} \State Ignore the first layer of $\theta$ \Comment{Adapt to new dataset dimensions} \For{$e \in\{1, \ldots, E\}$} \State Split $\mathcal{T}$ into $B$ batches $\{\mathcal{T}_1, \ldots, \mathcal{T}_B\}$ \Comment{Batch splitting process} \For{batch $b \in \{1, \ldots, B\}$} \State Extract batch $\left(x_b, y_b\right)$ from $\mathcal{T}_b$ \State $\mathbf{z}_0^b \gets f(x_b; \theta)$ \Comment{Fine-tuning} \State $\hat{y}_b \gets \Call{traverse}{(1, \mathbf{z}_0^b; \omega)}$ \Comment{Traverse the tree to get predictions} \State $\text{loss}(\hat{y}_b, y_b)$ \Comment{Calculate the loss based on predictions} \State Update $\theta$ and $\omega$ \Comment{Optimize model parameters using Adam optimizer} \EndFor \State Evaluate model on validation set $\mathcal{V}$ and obtain $val\_perf$ \If{$val\_perf > best\_val\_perf$} \State $best\_val\_perf \gets val\_perf$ \State $patience\_counter \gets 0$ \Comment{Reset patience counter} \Else \State $patience\_counter \gets patience\_counter + 1$ \EndIf \If{$patience\_counter > P$} \Comment{Early stopping condition} \State break \Comment{Stop training if no improvement} \EndIf \State \quad $learning\_rate$ $\gets$$ learning\_rate$ $\times$ $decay\_rate^{(e / decay\_steps)} $ \Comment {Update the optimizer with the new learning rate} \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-355: We proposed ST-Tree with Interpretability for multivariate time series classification, which consists of a time patch module and a neural tree module.
elements.sent-2411-356: Each of these nodes corresponds to a trained time series training prototype and is used to compute the similarity with the time series during the classification process.
elements.sent-2411-357: Finally, the leaf nodes perform label prediction on the multivariate time series by calculating branch routing scores through the leaf prediction module.
elements.sent-2411-358: Experimental evaluation on 10 UEA datasets demonstrated that the ST-Tree model has improved accuracy on the multivariate time series classification task.
elements.sent-2411-359: And we provided interpretability by visualizing the decision-making process on different datasets.
elements.sent-2411-360: In our proposed method, the use of a perfect binary tree may result in redundant nodes.
elements.sent-2411-361: Therefore, in future work, we can address this issue by applying pruning techniques to reduce redundancy.
elements.sent-2411-362: Pruning can simplify the model structure, improve computational efficiency, and enhance generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-363: By pruning, we can also eliminate irrelevant features and nodes, making the decision-making process clearer and more interpretable.
